learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of human rights laws (including anti-discrimination laws) and be able to apply them in domestic legal situations.
Determine the kinds of remedies that are available to defend human rights in a variety of domestic legal situations.
Appreciate the manner in which the law impacts upon vulnerable groups in Australia.
Work in a team to develop skills in human rights lawyering.
Apply a 'rights-based' approach to legal practice.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Practical/ Demonstration, Role play/ Simulation
assessments.task_description:
Students will participate in four (4) class activities that aim to develop and assess practical legal skills. They are:

Week 3: Client interviewing

Week 6: Form filling

Week 7: Conciliation conference

Week 12: Plain English legal drafting

Students are required to:

1. attend the class and participate in the activity

2. complete a reflection form and submit it via Blackboard by 1pm the Monday after that class. 

The reflection form should be completed in class, or immediately after the class.

Reflection forms will vary in length depending on the task, but as a rough guide, there should be around 200-300 of your own words. 

Reflection forms are available on Blackboard under ‘Learning Resources’.

Students will receive a grade of: 0 or 2.5. To receive 2.5, students must:

• genuinely reflect on the activity and make a reasonable attempt at answering the questions;

• write around 200-300 words for each form;

• not provide brief answers, or leave the answers blank;

• avoid using AI (there is no need to use it for this task), or acknowledge any use of AI on the reflection form.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will submit four (4) phone-a-friend templates.

Students phone-a-friend (partners allocated in class) on four occasions throughout the semester to discuss the readings for that week. Prompt questions (if needed) are: What were your impressions? What did you learn from the readings? What didn’t you understand? Did you agree/disagree with what the author said/judge decided?

The activity must be undertaken over the telephone. Not in person. Not via video conferencing.

Phone calls must be 20-30 minutes long.

Students can decide collaboratively which weeks they complete the exercise.

For each phone conversation, students must complete a Phone-a-Friend Template (on the next page). One Template must be completed for each phone conversation, so four (4) templates must be submitted by the due date via Turnitin.

Completing the template:

Your notes should be akin to a file note (ie. a contemporaneous record of a discussion or event). I expect it to be well-expressed and clear, but less formal than an essay. You should describe what you talked about, what your view was, what your partner’s view was and what conclusions you came to. It can be in point form. No formal references/footnotes are required.

For each entry, you must include: the date and time of the call; how long the call went for; your partner’s name. This is all provided for in the Template.

Make sure you include any challenges you faced with this task – eg. not being able to reach your partner to make a time; not being able to reach your partner at the agreed time. If you are not a collegial partner, this will affect your mark, so please make sure you work together on this, and be committed to making sure the task is completed by the due date.

Each week, your summary should be around 300 words long.

Please take your obligations to act professionally seriously. When you are admitted as a lawyer, you undertake to act as an officer of the court. This responsibility is taken seriously by the Legal Practitioners Admissions Board. Any offence or academic misconduct you commit can compromise your capacity to be admitted, or stay admitted as a legal practitioner. Professional honesty and integrity are fundamental professional values which must be respected now, and for the rest of your legal career. I am relying on you, and your partner, to be honest with me. Don’t agree to lie for anyone – it is not a good start to your career.

Students will receive a grade of: 0, 5 or 10. To receive a 10, students must:

• genuinely reflect on the readings with your friend and write detailed noted about what you discussed and the conclusions you came to;

• write around 300-400 words for each conversation;

• refer to the readings, however no formal referencing is required.

All four template forms must be submitted via the Blackboard site by Friday 30 October at 2pm.

assessments.due_date:
11/09/2026 - 14/09/2026

Students will book a time for their oral presentation on Friday 11 Sept, Sunday 13 Sept or Monday 14 Sept.
assessments.task_description:
Students will deliver an oral presentation in a private meeting with Prof Walsh. The presentation will be recorded.

Students will present an opening address for a client. Facts will be provided well in advance.

The opening address will be no more than 5 minutes’ duration. Students will then be asked one or two questions to demonstrate their understanding of the case.

Students must submit a script – a written version of their opening address – at the meeting. This does not have to be a word-for-word version of the presentation. It can be a series of dot points, for example. The script should not be longer than 750 words, and should not exceed two pages.

Students will be invited to book a timeslot with Prof Walsh on: Friday 11 September, Sunday 13 September of Monday 14 September.

Students can receive a maximum mark of 30. The oral presentation is worth 30% of students’ final grade.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 13(03 Aug - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

The seminar topics are:

Week 1 - Overview of the course | Video only (no classes)

Week 2 - Human rights law: Who needs it?

Week 3 - International human rights law: Historic human rights instruments and taking a rights-based approach

Week 4 - Rights in Australian law (Commonwealth)

Week 5 - Human Rights Acts in Australia

Week 6 - Human Rights in Queensland: Rights in Australian law

Week 7 - Human rights dispute resolution

Week 8 - Oral presentations (no classes)

Week 9 - Introduction to Anti-Discrimination Law | Video only (no classes): International approaches to human rights law

Week 10 - Race discrimination and human rights of Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander Peoples

Week 11 - Sex discrimination and sexual harassment

Week 12 - Disability discrimination: The Victorian Charter

Week 13 - Other grounds of discrimination


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
